issues.description: MONITORING TAG ERROR: WIN-060 code_analysis.monitoring is "hardcoded" but no hardcoded monitoring check exists for this WIN. The dome's W022 prediction maps to WIN-040 (static position), not WIN-060 (drift direction). The live dashboard SAA domain tracks intensity decay rate (nT/yr), not drift direction. All sibling WINs (040, 041, 043, 059, 063) are correctly tagged "none". WIN-060 should be "none" too.

KILL SHOT 6 USES UNSOURCED 30% THRESHOLD: sections.json part5 (Kill Shots) says "the dome's methodology page states that predictions exceeding 30% error trigger review and potential falsification." The actual dome criterion is 50% (dashboard shows PASS at 39.9%). The 30% figure does not appear in the extracted dome raw-text. Also incorrectly labels the result "self-falsifying" when the dome marks it PASS. Cross-cutting error: both sections.json part5 and previously WIN-059 detail_evidence referenced this unsourced threshold (WIN-059 was already fixed; part5 still has it).

UNSOURCED 40% POPULATION CLAIM: detail_evidence states "~40% of the world's population" lives in the ±12° equatorial band excluded by V13. The tropics (±23.4°) contain ~40%; the ±12° band is roughly half that latitude range and contains far fewer people (~15-20%). Either cite a source or use a more conservative estimate.

SELF-FALSIFIED FRAMING INACCURATE: detail_evidence says "By the dome model's own published falsification criteria, WIN-059 is self-falsified." But the dome's own dashboard shows this test as PASS (50% tolerance, 39.9% error). The correct framing is tolerance-gaming, not self-falsification. Should be: "The dome marks this as PASS by using a 50% tolerance that was widened from an originally tighter value after predictions missed."

POLE APERTURE RADIUS ARBITRARY: The area ratio derivation uses a pole aperture radius without sourcing where this value comes from. The choice appears arbitrary — if it is dome-defined, cite the dome parameter; if we chose it for the calculation, acknowledge that.

FINDING CONFLATES TWO CRITIQUES: "El Gordo is a genuine 6.2σ ΛCDM anomaly" (acknowledging a real problem) and "Citing a competitor's unsolved problem without offering your own solution is non-discriminating" (cherry-picking critique). These are not compatible: if El Gordo is genuinely anomalous, the dome is not cherry-picking; if it's cherry-picking, then El Gordo is not genuinely hard for ΛCDM. The finding should clearly commit to one framing.

NUMERICAL INCONSISTENCY: detail_evidence says "600–700× less accurate" while comparing to "sub-0.01%" elsewhere. The 7.3% NH regression vs sub-0.01% gives ~730×; the 6.2% RMSE gives ~620×. The range "600–700×" is reasonable but should be clearly explained: these represent best-case (6.2% RMSE) and worst-case (7.3% NH) relative to Vincenty at sub-0.01%.

MISSING OVERLAP DISTINCTION: WIN-004 (separation), WIN-005 (decay rate), and WIN-060 (drift direction) share the same INTERMAGNET/Swarm dataset. The text says they overlap without explaining why this constitutes redundancy rather than independent tests. Should add: "independent properties read from the same dataset without independent dome-geometric derivation does not multiply predictive power."

MOND PRIORITY ARGUMENT UNDERREPRESENTED: detail_verdict_text mentions MOND once but buries it. Must explicitly contrast MOND's mathematical content (transition function μ(x), critical acceleration a₀) against dome's zero-equation aetheric label. A dome defender can say "if MOND explains RAR without dark matter, why not aetheric medium?" — the counterargument must be prominent.

GALAXY-SCALE ONTOLOGY UNDERPLAYED: The firmament-at-9,086-km structural incoherence is mentioned once as a secondary point. This is the single strongest argument — the dome cannot physically accommodate galaxies at kpc distances. Should lead the rebuttal, not be buried.

Cross-WIN structural incoherence (WINs 047, 048, 052) not explicitly called out: these WINs test the same underlying claim (RAR/galaxy dynamics) from different angles without independent dome-geometric derivations. A reviewer should note this overlap explicitly.

MOND vs ΛCDM framing confusion: rebuttal text conflates two separate arguments — (1) MOND has priority over dome on the RAR, and (2) ΛCDM's difficulties don't help the dome. These should be clearly separated.

WIN-055 and WIN-047 duplication: both WINs test essentially the same observable (distance ladder calibration) from overlapping data. This redundancy has not been explicitly called out in the text despite being flagged in Cycle 1.

MISSING CROSS-REFERENCE: detail_evidence for WIN-053 makes a claim that is supported by analysis in Section 6.8 (old 3.5.8) on flux conservation. The cross-reference is absent, leaving the argument without backup.

WIN-061 citation imprecise: detail_evidence cites Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 ch.11 for storm-specific Schumann suppression evidence, but Schlegel & Füllekrug (1999, J. Geophys. Res. 104, 10111) is the primary peer-reviewed source specifically documenting G3+ storm suppression of Schumann frequencies. The general reference should be supplemented or replaced with the storm-specific one.

WIN-061 Live Power Dashboard untapped: the dome's own Live Power Dashboard lists WIN-061 as "Unscored — no G3+ storm observed." This is a powerful self-undermining argument: the dome claims confirmation of a G3+ storm suppression test, but its own monitoring infrastructure confirms no qualifying storm has been observed. detail_evidence should add: "The dome's own Live Power Dashboard scores this WIN as Unscored — meaning by its own automated metrics, the test has never been run."

EXHIBIT-AB COVERAGE GAP: The dome's home page prominently features Evidence Exhibits A and B (2003 Bedrock Audit of BOU station and Administrative Mask Exposure). These are the first things visitors see, yet our review has no dedicated section addressing them. Visitors who arrive from the dome site's home page will find our rebuttal silent on the dome's most prominent claims.

EXHIBIT-AB STATISTICAL CLAIMS UNADDRESSED: The dome's Exhibit A prominently cites r=0.992 correlation and AI=1.152 asymmetry index as key evidence. These specific statistics are never addressed in our review. Any reader familiar with the dome site will notice this gap.

Kim et al. citation in detail_evidence is listed as 2021 (ApJ 923, 101) — Cycle 1 raised concern about this citation's accuracy. The DOI (10.3847/1538-4357/ac294f) should be verified to confirm it corresponds to a paper about El Gordo mass measurements and that the mass figure cited (2.13×10^15 M_sun) appears in that paper.
issues.fix_details: DOI verified correct: 10.3847/1538-4357/ac294f is Kim et al. 2021 ApJ 923, 101 (Head-to-Toe Measurement of El Gordo). Paper is about El Gordo mass. Risk low per investigation notes — our argument does not hinge on the specific mass value.
issues.investigation_notes: DOI 10.3847/1538-4357/ac294f VERIFIED: IS Kim et al. 2021 ApJ 923, 101 (Head-to-Toe Measurement of El Gordo). IS about El Gordo cluster. Their reported subcluster masses: M_NW=9.9e14 + M_SE=6.5e14 ≈ 1.64e15 M_sun combined. The 2.13e15 M_sun figure cited in our text may be from the total system mass measurement in the same paper. DOI is correct; paper is correct; mass figure needs full text verification. Risk low — our argument does not hinge on the specific mass value.

The 5 entries are flagged needs_reconciliation:true and excluded from the new headline silent-failures counter until reconciled. TASK: For each of FAIL-001..005, trace the original prediction through V50.6 (raw-text-v50.6-2026-03-12/) and the current site (raw-text/) to determine its current state — is it still on the site under a different W-number? Moved to OPEN-NNN bucket? Marked refined or removed inline? Vanished entirely? FAIL-001 references "OPEN-006 data" suggesting the dome has a separate OPEN-NNN bucket we have not audited — please survey it as part of this work. SCHEMA REDESIGN: The current single dome_ref field cannot capture the full history. Design a new field structure that captures THREE names: (1) current_ref — what the dome calls it now, with "none" as a valid value if vanished; (2) prior_ref — the last known dome reference before reuse; (3) prediction_name — what the prediction was originally registered as (e.g. PROS-003 → WIN-010 promotion path). The dome_status_current field already exists for the silent-failures bucket and can be reused. ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION: WIN-058b in our wins.json is suspected to share the same root cause — the dome reused/renumbered around it, and we improvised a sub-id workaround. The integrity report previously flagged a CA_PENDING off-by-one tied to the 058b sub-id. Please investigate whether 058b is another instance of the dome-reuse pattern and whether the new schema would let us remove the workaround. DELIVERABLE: Updated uncounted-failures.json with reconciled entries, new schema applied to all entries, needs_reconciliation flags cleared, and a brief writeup of the WIN-058b investigation. After reconciliation, the headline silent-failures counter on the wins tab will automatically include any reconciled items where current_ref shows the dome no longer counts them.

issues.title: Post-hoc tag: caveat with author self-definition and carve out legitimate novelty
issues.description: Dome author home-page defines prospective as "wrote the prediction down before I pulled the data" — a workflow-local definition, NOT the scientific standard ("observation did not exist at prediction time, or predictor provably had no knowledge of it, with timestamped pre-registration"). We have been holding him to the scientific standard without addressing his stated one. Two required methodology changes: (1) Every WIN rebuttal currently tagged post_hoc: true must explicitly cite the author’s definition and explain why it falls short — not just assert post-hoc. (2) Methodology section needs a caveat paragraph explaining the distinction and defining where we draw the legitimate-novelty line.

position.legitimate_novelty: If the author had a genuinely unique theoretical framework and did NOT happen to know the matching data existed, that is a legitimate independent discovery. Cross-field rediscovery happens constantly in science — researchers in field A don’t know about existing results in field B. Under that scenario, "I wrote it before I pulled the data" IS a valid prospective claim, provided (a) the prediction is non-trivial and specific, and (b) the matching value is not common knowledge or trivially lookup-able.
position.illegitimate_transcription: What is NOT legitimate: predicting a value that is already widely published and trivially lookup-able, then claiming prospective validation because you personally hadn’t run the fetch script yet. Human analogy: "The sky is blue, but I didn’t validate the Pantone; I predict Pantone 2727C; now I validate and yes, it is Pantone 2727C." If the Pantone is in any design textbook, the prediction is transcription, not discovery.
position.the_test: The distinguishing test for each post_hoc WIN: is the specific numerical value in question (a) obscure enough to plausibly be unknown to someone working outside the relevant field, AND (b) specific enough that getting it right is informative? If yes, the claim could be a legitimate independent rediscovery (even if the data existed). If the value is in every physics textbook (Schumann 7.83 Hz, G = 6.67e-11, lunar distance), the prospective claim collapses to transcription.
position.the_smoking_gun: The author’s OWN monitor scripts typically hardcode the expected value in the same file as the live fetch. There is no git-timestamped pre-registration separating prediction from observation. That makes even the author’s weaker self-definition unverifiable — there is no way to prove which came first. Rebuttals should cite this directly when applicable: "You say prospective means before you pulled the data; your code commits show the expected value was added in the same commit as the fetch call, so there is no evidence of prediction preceding observation."

tasks: 1. Write a methodology caveat paragraph for the prose section that currently introduces post_hoc reasoning. Cite the author’s home-page definition verbatim (need URL + quote), explain our stricter standard, define the legitimate-novelty carve-out, define the trivial-transcription test.

2. Audit every WIN currently tagged post_hoc: true. For each, check whether the rebuttal text addresses the author’s definition or just asserts post-hoc. Where it does not, add a sentence citing the author’s definition and explaining why this specific value fails the novelty or pre-registration test.

3. For each post_hoc WIN, classify the value itself: common-knowledge (textbook), field-specific published, obscure. Common-knowledge cases should note that explicitly. Obscure cases may actually strengthen the author’s case — flag for verdict review.

4. Check dome repo git history for genuine pre-registration: are there commits where the expected value was added BEFORE the fetch call was added? If yes, those WINs may deserve different verdict treatment. If no (expected given what we have seen of inject_ai_layer.py etc.), that is a broadly-applicable rebuttal we can cite systematically.
5. Cross-reference with ISS-677 (Prospective Wins Mode 5) — whatever criteria we adopt there must use the same novelty/pre-registration standard this issue establishes.
